title: Research Series Examines Stability of Funding Models

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York released a three-part Economic Policy Review series examining the structure and stability of different funding models for financial intermediation, including a literature review, crisis case studies, and an analytical framework for evaluating funding model resilience during stress events.
key_points: Series comprises three reports: literature review, crisis case studies, and an analytical framework by New York Fed researchers
First report reviews economics literature on stability of banks and financial intermediaries and their role as liquidity providers
Second report presents case studies of markets and intermediaries that experienced distress during the crisis, covering sources of disruption and policy responses
Third report provides framework to evaluate how funding model characteristics affect survival during stress events
Authors include Tanju Yorulmazer (research officer), Thomas Eisenbach (economist), Todd Keister (Rutgers professor), and James McAndrews (EVP and director of research)
Framework applied to policy issues including liquidity regulation, discount window policies, and money market mutual fund reforms
why_it_matters: This research series provides foundational analysis on how different funding models contribute to financial system fragility and resilience. The analytical framework remains relevant for evaluating regulatory proposals and understanding systemic risk in financial intermediation.
market_structure_context: Research examines funding model fragility, liquidity provision roles of intermediaries, and how policy choices (liquidity regulation, discount window, money market reform) affect financial stability—directly relevant to market plumbing and structural resilience.

issues_found: Source is historical (published 2014, over 12 years old) and may have outdated policy references; confidence slightly lowered per freshness guidelines
Content is a press release announcing research rather than the full research papers themselves; limited detail on specific findings
Does not contain actionable market signals or current market data

human_review.notes: Historical research series announcement from 2014. Relevant to market structure topic but dated. No current market impact.

notes: Source is a historical research announcement (2014) from New York Fed's Economic Policy Review series. While relevant to market structure analysis of funding model stability, the content is limited to announcing the series rather than presenting full research findings. Authors and institutional context are credible. Consider seeking actual research papers for deeper analysis.
